title: Quick check, Fortification and Web Apps

questions.prompt: Why does ApisCP run PHP under a separate 'apache' user instead of the account's admin user?
choices: Performance optimisation; the apache user has higher CPU priority
Privilege separation; if a PHP app is compromised, the attacker has apache's permissions only, not the account user's
Logging; PHP logs to a different file when run as apache
It's a legacy decision from earlier ApisCP versions, kept for backwards compatibility
questions.explanation: Fortification's core idea is that a compromised web app shouldn't compromise the whole account. PHP runs as apache; the account user owns the core files; apache can write only to specified paths.

questions.prompt: Update Assurance takes a snapshot before every Web App update so a failed update can roll back automatically.
questions.explanation: Pre-update snapshot + post-update health check = automatic rollback on failure. This is what makes platform-wide scheduled updates safe to run unattended.
